text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 22 September 2025 

Dr Priya Menon
Southbank Medical Centre

221 Borough High Street, London SE1 1GA
Dear Dr Priya Menon
Michael Carter, DOB: 04-02-1989, Hospital No: Q983772 NHS No. 7426159380
Flat 3, 9 Millstream Court, London, SE1 6PT 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. He attended alone and was able to give a clear and consistent account. He has practiced aura recognition strategies previously and is able to describe his warning features in his own words; he reports a brief sensation of “a rising flicker in the chest and a momentary blankness” that used to precede his events by seconds, occasionally accompanied by a metallic taste. He has kept a personal log of these sensations and has not documented any such episodes for a prolonged period. He previously suffered from frequent clusters of myoclonic jerks, absence seizures and occasional convulsive events two times per year, but is currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years. 

He has not required emergency care or rescue medication in this interval, and there have been no reported injuries. He continues in full-time employment and is driving within DVLA guidance, having met the requisite seizure-free period; he confirms no lapses in consciousness or awareness. Sleep is regular and he minimises missed doses; he uses smartphone reminders. He denies mood disturbance, though notes occasional fatigue after long shifts, which he does not associate with any neurological symptoms. There is no history of recent head injury, intercurrent infection, or alcohol/drug misuse. 

Medications: He remains stable on Levetiracetam 1000 mg b.d. and Lamotrigine 150 mg b.d., both well tolerated without rash, behavioural change, or cognitive complaints. He reports no auras on this regimen. No other regular medications, and no known drug allergies. 

Examination today was unremarkable: he was alert and oriented with intact language and memory on bedside testing; cranial nerves normal; no tremor, ataxia, or focal neurological deficits; gait steady. Bloods from his GP six weeks ago (U&E, LFT, FBC, Vitamin D) were within normal limits per the shared record. 

We reviewed his self-management plan. He remains confident recognising his historical warning features, should they recur, and would rest, avoid precipitants, and alert a family member. He understands when to seek urgent care. We discussed ongoing adherence, sleep hygiene, and avoidance of known triggers (sleep deprivation, binge alcohol). He is aware to inform DVLA and our team if any loss of awareness or myoclonic clusters recur. 

Plan:
- Continue Levetiracetam 1000 mg b.d. and Lamotrigine 150 mg b.d. unchanged.
- No additional investigations indicated at present given sustained remission and absence of red flags; will reconsider if symptoms recur.
- Provide written seizure first-aid refresh and safety advice (bathroom/kitchen precautions, sports safeguards) for his records.
- Routine follow-up in 12 months, with open access to bring this forward if any warning features, myoclonic jerks, absences, or convulsive events occur.

I have copied this letter to the patient for information. Please let me know if there are any concerns from the primary care perspective.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years

He previously suffered from frequent clusters of myoclonic jerks, absence seizures and occasional convulsive events two times per year, but is currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the patient is currently in long-term remission and has been seizure free for years. It also confirms no lapses in consciousness or awareness and no auras on current regimen. The duration is described as 'for years', which satisfies the requirement of at least 6 months and qualifies as seizure free for years. No more specific numeric duration is provided, so use 'multiple years'.
seizure_frequency_number: currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years
reference: He previously suffered from frequent clusters of myoclonic jerks, absence seizures and occasional convulsive events two times per year, but is currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years

flaws.description: Gold (seizure free for multiple year) disagrees with Reference (seizure free for multiple month)
